content.problemStatement:
We have taken a great initiative to have a women only quota for admissions. The admission criteria is to solve one graph problem. Alice, being a woman, seeks admission under this quota and wants your help to solve the following problem.

You are given an integer NNN. Construct a good graph with NNN vertices.

A good graph is one that satisfies the following conditions:


The graph is simple (i.e, doesn't contain self-loops or multiedges), undirected, and **connected**.
If ‚ą£i‚ąíj‚ą£|i-j|‚ą£i‚ąíj‚ą£ is a prime number, then degi‚Č†degjdeg_i \neq deg_jdegi‚ÄčÓÄ†=degj‚Äč, where degideg_idegi‚Äč is the degree of vertex iii.
Among all graphs on NNN vertices that satisfy the above two conditions, a good graph is one that then **minimizes the maximum degree**, i.e, max‚Ā°i=1Ndegi\max_{i=1}^N deg_imaxi=1N‚Äčdegi‚Äč is minimized.
If such a graph exists, find any one of them. If no such graph exists, print ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the number of nodes in the graph.


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer as follows:



If no good graph on NNN vertices exists, print ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 on a new line.
Otherwise, first print a single integer XXX: the maximum degree of a good graph on NNN vertices.
On the next line, print a single integer MMM: the number of edges in the graph you construct.
Then, print MMM lines. The iii-th of these lines should contain two space-separated integers uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč, denoting an edge between uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč.

The graph you print should satisfy all the conditions laid out in the statement.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: N=2N = 2N=2, and we have deg1=deg2=1deg_1 = deg_2 = 1deg1‚Äč=deg2‚Äč=1 which is the best we can do.
Test case 222: N=3N = 3N=3, and we have deg1=deg2=1deg_1 = deg_2 = 1deg1‚Äč=deg2‚Äč=1 and deg3=2deg_3 = 2deg3‚Äč=2. ‚ą£1‚ąí3‚ą£=2|1-3| = 2‚ą£1‚ąí3‚ą£=2 is prime, and 111 and 333 have different degrees as required.
Test case 333: N=4N = 4N=4, and we have deg1=deg2=2deg_1 = deg_2 = 2deg1‚Äč=deg2‚Äč=2, deg3=deg4=1deg_3 = deg_4 = 1deg3‚Äč=deg4‚Äč=1. It's easy to verify that any pair with a difference of 222 or 333 have different degrees.
